# SR 26-2 — Guidance on Model Risk Management (2026)

> SYNTHETIC STAND-IN for local development and tests. Not the real Supervisory
> Letter. Replace with the actual SR 26-2 text in docs/governance/.

## I. Purpose and scope

This guidance sets supervisory expectations for the management of model risk at
banking organizations. Traditional machine-learning models used in credit and
fraud decisioning remain fully within the scope of this guidance. Generative and
agentic AI systems are addressed separately in Section VII.

## II. Definition of model risk

Model risk is the potential for adverse consequences from decisions based on
incorrect or misused model outputs. It arises from fundamental model errors and
from use of a model outside the environment for which it was designed.

## III. Ongoing monitoring

### III.A Monitoring expectations

Ongoing monitoring is essential to evaluate whether a model continues to perform
as intended. Banks should monitor model inputs and outputs for population drift
and performance decay on a frequency commensurate with the model's materiality.
For high-materiality fraud models, monitoring should be performed daily.

### III.B Population stability and thresholds

Institutions should track the stability of the score distribution against a
frozen reference using an appropriate metric such as the Population Stability
Index. A material shift in the score distribution near the decision threshold
warrants investigation even when the aggregate stability metric appears benign,
because aggregate measures can mask deterioration localized at the operating
point. Score shifts toward the high end of the distribution typically indicate
increased flagging and a rising rate of false declines; shifts toward the low
end indicate weakening detection and elevated fraud and regulatory exposure.

### III.C Escalation

Monitoring results that breach established thresholds should be escalated to
model risk management and the relevant business owner. Escalation criteria and
the responsible parties should be defined in advance.

## IV. Performance and outcome analysis

Banks should compare model predictions against realized outcomes, tracking
precision, recall, and the false-positive rate. Sustained deterioration in these
measures is itself a trigger for investigation independent of input drift.

## V. Fairness and disparate outcomes

Where a model bears on customers, institutions should assess whether error
rates differ materially across protected or sensitive groups, and document the
rationale for any disparity that cannot be remediated.

## VI. Governance, roles, and documentation

Every model decision and monitoring result that informs an action should be
attributable, documented, and retained in a manner that supports independent
review. Records should be complete enough to reconstruct what was decided, on
what basis, and by whom.

## VII. Generative and agentic AI

Generative and agentic AI systems are novel and rapidly evolving and are placed
outside the scope of traditional model-risk guidance. Institutions deploying
such systems are expected to govern them under their own risk-management
practices, including clearly defined permitted actions, mandatory human approval
before any consequential action, and a complete and immutable activity record.
